title: Striga Witchweed
symptoms: Also known as purple witchweed, this plant is characterized by green stems and leaves as well as small, bright purple flowers. The weed parasitizes crops and sucks water and nutrients from the host plant, leading to symptoms similar to drought or nutrient deficiency: yellowing, wilting leaves, and stunted growth. Symptoms before the weed appears are difficult to diagnose accurately because they resemble common nutrient deficiencies. Once witchweed appears, it is too late to mitigate the damage, even by removing the weed. This can result in significant yield losses.
organic_control: Witchweed is one of the most difficult parasitic plants to control, mainly due to the high number of seeds it produces and its lifespan. Affected plants should be uprooted and burned before the weed flowers. The fungus Fusarium oxysporum can be used as a biological control method against witchweed because the fungus infects the new vascular tissues of the Striga plant and impairs its growth.
chemical_control: Always consider an integrated approach combining preventative measures with biological treatments if available. Available witchweed herbicides are quite expensive and can also directly affect crops. Sprayers need to be protected and herbicides can kill beneficial plants. Seed herbicides have been successfully applied to millet and sorghum with weed infestation rates reduced by up to 80%. The method involves soaking herbicide-resistant seeds in a herbicide solution before planting.
cause: Symptoms caused by The parasitic plant Striga hermonthica, commonly known as purple witch weed or giant witch weed, is a particularly serious problem in Sub-Saharan Africa. It can be a dangerous problem for cereal crops, rice, and legumes including rice, corn, millet, sorghum, sugarcane, and string beans. Each witch weed plant can produce between 90,000 and 500,000 seeds, which can survive in the soil for over 10 years. These seeds overwinter in the soil after being dispersed by wind, water, animals, or human machinery. When favorable weather conditions arise, and when they are a few centimeters from the base of the host plant, they begin to germinate. Upon contact with the roots, the witch weed creates a structure that attaches it to the host plant, thereby establishing a parasitic relationship. Nitrogen-rich fertilizers also reduce witch weed infestation rates.
prevention: Using plant growth regulators to enhance plant health. Apply plenty of manure early in the season. | Sow Semolina plants in the fields to prevent witches' leaf spot from attaching to crop roots. | Planting elephant grass around the fields also helps pull witches' leaf spot away from main crops. | Clean all tools, equipment, clothing, and shoes carefully after working with infected plants. | Apply appropriate nitrogen fertilizer. | Plan your crop rotation with legumes.
summary: A plant with bright green leaves and small, vibrant purple flowers that grows around the field. | Symptoms of the host plant are stunting, wilting, and fading.

title: Fruit Cracking Disease in Tomatoes
symptoms: Symptoms appear as cracks on the outer skin of the fruit. The cracks vary in depth and size, usually appearing around the top of the fruit. The concentric or radial symmetry of the lesions may indicate different physiological problems the plant is experiencing. Sometimes the bottom of the fruit is also affected. The younger the fruit, the greater the damage caused by the cracks. Cracks may appear around the stem. This disorder is caused by the fruit growing excessively beyond the capacity of the fruit's skin. The elasticity of the fruit's skin is subjected to excessive tension, causing small cracks to appear and gradually break.Cracking out.
organic_control: There are no biological controls for this disease. It can only be treated with preventative measures.
chemical_control: Always consider the possibility of combining preventative and biological treatments, to the extent applicable. This disease can only be treated with preventative measures. At the same time, avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen and pay attention to the potassium content in the soil.
cause: Cracking occurs due to the fruit developing too quickly and suddenly, often associated with the plant absorbing excess water. Such sudden changes in plant development can be triggered or exacerbated by changes in environmental conditions such as wet and cold weather, high humidity, following periods of hot and dry weather. A balanced fertilization regime is also important to help the plant avoid this situation. For example: over-fertilizing with nitrogen and under-fertilizing with potassium during the flowering and fruiting stages.This is a factor that causes excessive fruit growth, leading to the appearance of cracks.
prevention: Choose tomato varieties with high resistance to cracking. | Avoid overwatering; aim for a stable water supply to the garden instead of sudden flooding. | Pay attention to upcoming extreme weather events and conditions of high humidity and low sunlight. | Maintain a stable balance between the number of leaves and fruit on each plant to avoid stressing the plant. | At the same time, avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen and under-fertilizing with potassium.
summary: Excessive stress on the fruit skin causes cracks to appear. | Cracks can also appear around the stem.

title: Tomato Catface Disease
symptoms: Tomato Catface is a physiological disorder that causes the fruit to develop abnormally with the formation of scars, usually appearing at the end of the flowering stage. Affected fruits often have a lobed shape with brown scars appearing between the lobes and potentially penetrating deep into the flesh. This symptom should not be confused with concentric or radial fruit cracking in tomato fruit cracking disease. Although the commercial value is lost, such deformed fruits retain their normal flavor and are completely safe to use. Possible causes of this symptom include... The above is due to cold weather, with nighttime temperatures below 12°C, occurring during the flowering stage, high nitrogen content, and herbicide damage. Tomato varieties that produce large fruits are more susceptible to this disease than others.
organic_control: This disease can only be controlled by preventative measures.
chemical_control: Always consider the possibility of combining preventative measures and biological treatments, where applicable. This disease can only be controlled by easily implemented preventative measures. On the other hand, avoid using herbicides that can trigger conditions leading to this disease in susceptible tomato varieties.
cause: The exact cause of tomato fruit malformation is not yet determined, but generally the symptoms mentioned above appear more frequently in tomato varieties that produce large fruits. There is a correlation between prolonged low nighttime temperatures (below 12°C) and other factors. For several consecutive days during the bud development stage, this physiological disorder may be due to incomplete pollination. Some tomato varieties are more sensitive to such prolonged temperature changes than others. Conditions that hinder bud development also lead to deformed fruit. Mechanical damage to the flowers, excessive pruning, or exposure to certain herbicides (2,4-D) can also lead to deformed fruit. Finally, damage caused by thrips or a condition called tomato stunting syndrome can also lead to deformed fruit.
prevention: Use varieties with high tolerance to temperature changes. | Avoid using herbicides as this can lead to this disease. | Regularly monitor the temperature during the bud development stage. | Avoid causing mechanical damage to the plants during garden work. | Maintain a regular schedule.Monitor nitrogen levels in the soil before fertilizing.
summary: Fruits begin to develop deformities with severe scarring and cracks at the end of the flowering stage.

title: Tomato Scarring Disease
symptoms: Scarring is a physiological disorder resulting in the formation of thin, brown necrotic scars running along the length of the fruit, creating localized cracks. The scars develop partly along the length and width of the fruit, sometimes connecting the bottom of the fruit to the stem in a zipper-like lesion. Deep grooves in the scar tissue, deforming the fruit surface, are also common symptoms. Damaged tissues lose their elasticity and the fruit cannot develop properly. Once the damage has occurred, any treatment is too late.
organic_control: There are no biological controls for this disease. It can only be treated with preventative measures.
chemical_control: If possible, always consider a combination of preventative and biological treatments. This disease can only be treated with preventative measures.
cause: Scarring is a physiological disorder caused by low temperature and high humidity conditions at the end of the flowering and beginning of the fruiting stage. During the development of young fruit, one or more anthers remain attached to the ovary wall, creating small scars on the outside of the fruit as it grows. Temperature sensitivity varies depending on the tomato variety. Some tomato varieties are more sensitive than others to temperature changes, with the Beefsteak tomato variety being one of the least tolerant.
prevention: Plant varieties that are resistant to cold weather. | Ensure optimal environmental conditions for the plants during the flowering and fruiting stages. | Avoid exposing the plants to low temperatures, excessive dryness, or excessive humidity at night. | Remove scarred fruit early to help the plant conserve energy and nutrients.
summary: Characteristic thin necrotic scars run along the stem of the fruit. | The scars can spread throughout the entire fruit.The length of the fruit extends to the base. | This disorder occurs due to low temperature and high humidity conditions during the fruiting stage of the plant.

title: Alternaria Leaf Blight
symptoms: Young plants are resistant to the pathogen. The lowermost leaves are always the first to show signs of infection, which gradually spreads to the upper leaves. Infection begins as small, oval, chlorotic lesions which are irregularly scattered on the lowermost leaves, gradually spreading to the upper leaves. Over time, the lesions enlarge, become irregular in shape and develop into dark brown or grey sunken lesions. Lesions may have a bright yellow marginal zone and can grow up to 1cm or more in diameter. Under moist conditions, lesions may be covered by black powdery conidia. At later stages of the disease, lesions will merge together resulting in the death of the entire leaf. In severe cases, leaf sheaths, awns and glumes are also affected and have a burnt appearance.
organic_control: Seedborne infections can be controlled by treating seed with vitavax @ 2.5 per kg seed. A mixture of Trichoderma viride and vitavax effectively hinder further infections (by 98.4%). Mix Urea @ 2 - 3% with Zineb at first and second sprays. Apply aqueous Neem Leaf extracts. Fungicide and hot water treatments have been used to reduce seedborne inoculum. Pathogens Trichoderma viride (2%) and T. harzianum(2%), Aspergillus humicola and Bacillus subtilis are often used to control further outbreak of the disease.
chemical_control: Always consider an integrated approach with preventive measures together with biological treatments, if available. A. triticina can be controlled through the application of fungicides with a 75% reduction in disease severity and an increase in plant yield. Apply fungicides such as mancozeb, ziram, zineb (0.2%), thiram, fytolan, propineb, chlorothalonil and nabam, propiconazole (0.15%), tebuconazole, and hexaconazole (0.5%). To prevent developing tolerance to mancozeb, apply a combination of fungicides.
cause: Damage is caused by the fungus Alternaria triticina. Infection is both soilborne and seedborne and can be dispersed by wind. Infected seeds may be smaller than healthy ones and are often shriveled, with brown discoloration. Plants are affected when planted into infected soils or getting in contact with infested crop residues (e.g., by rain splashes or by direct contact). The fungus survives reportedly two months in infected debris on the soil surface during summer, but four months in buried debris. Susceptibility increases with plant age as A. triticina is unable to infect young wheat seedlings which are under about four weeks of age. The symptoms are usually not evident until the plants are about seven weeks of age. Temperatures of 20 - 25°C are optimal for infection and disease development. Under severe conditions, yield losses may exceed 80%.
prevention: Use less-susceptible varieties as well as healthy seeds from certified sources. | Practice the best hygiene measures. | Infected crops should be destroyed by burning and ploughing. | This will prevent aerial dispersal of the pathogen via infected crop residues. | Herbicides can be used to destroy the infected crops. | Infested fields should not be resown for a minimum of two years. | Disposable equipment, infected plant material or soil should be disposed of by autoclaving, high-temperature incineration or deep burial. | Any equipment removed from the site for disposal should be double-bagged. | Deeply incorporate crop debris after harvest to reduce pathogen survival and primary inoculum that could incite future epidemics. | Promote air movement within the canopy by planting rows parallel to prevailing wind direction, reducing plant population and planting on wider row spacing. | Avoid irrigating near dusk and following overhead irrigation, if possible to reduce periods of leaf wetness. | Apply adequate fertilizers.
summary: The disease appears when wheat plants are 7-8 weeks old. | The condition becomes severe when the crop is mature. | Small, oval, discolored lesions can be found scattered on the leaves in an irregular manner. | As the lesions enlarge, they become dark brown to grey and irregular in shape.

title: Rose Mildew
symptoms: This disease is characterized by the dense, persistent and grayish patches of fungal growth on leaves, stems, inflorescences and fruits. Over time, the powdery coating causes the distortion and discoloration of leaves and premature leaf fall in severe cases. Buds often fail to unfold normally or be destroyed. Twigs may show a stunted growth. Fruits develop discoloration and lesions that prevents further normal development. In case of severe infections, the skin eventually turns dark brown, with the surface becoming leathery and hard, often subjected to cracks. Hosts include apricot, sour cherries and peach.
organic_control: Products based on the antagonist fungus Ampelomyces quisqualis and the bacteria Bacillus subtilis have been used as biological agents to control Podosphaera pannosa. Several organic fungicides are also available in the form of horticultural oils, neem oil, jojoba oil, potassium bicarbonate and bicarbonate of soda.
chemical_control: Always consider an integrated approach with preventive measures and biological treatments if available. Formulations based on thiophanate-methyl, propiconazole and sulfur fungicides have been recommended for powdery mildews in general, including powdery mildew of rose. Other products that have been used with some success include dinitrophenols and  compounds based on pyridine and pyrimidine.
cause: The disease is caused on roses and stone fruits by the fungus Podosphaera pannosa, which survives the winter in a circular, spore-producing structure. Under favorable conditions in spring, this structure breaks open and releases spores that are later spread by wind, insects, and rain. When they land on a susceptible host and germinates, they start producing the characteristic “powdery” appearance on leaves or fruits. Optimal conditions for powdery mildew of rose are 16-27 °C, with the optimal temperature for fungal growth at 23 °C. Shaded areas are also preferred since the prolonged exposure to light reduces its growth. Moreover, these fungi do not need moisture to germinate and infect their hosts. In fact, prolonged leaf moisture inhibits rather than promotes fungal growth. The use of silicon as part of a fertilization program can also reduce the occurrence of powdery mildew.
prevention: The selection of resistant varieties is crucial to avoid fungal growth and development of symptoms. | Monitor the orchards for signs of the disease. | Choose location well exposed to the sun and with good aeration. | Complement the fertilization program with silicon. | Avoid irrigating in late afternoon or evening. | Keep weeds and grasses under control under trees with low hanging branches. | Prune trees in a way that allow a good ventilation of the canopy.
summary: Dense, persistent and grayish patches of fungal growth on leaves, stems, inflorescences and fruits. | Powdery coating causes the formation of lesions, distortion and discoloration of leaves and fruits. | In case of severe infections, the fruit skin eventually turns dark brown, and cracks may appear.

title: Apple Scabies
symptoms: The first visible symptoms of apple scab appear in spring as small, circular, olive-green spots on the leaves, typically along the main veins. As these spots spread, they turn brown-black and eventually merge into large necrotic areas. Infected leaves often become distorted and fall prematurely, leading to a sparse canopy in severe cases. On shoots, the affected areas swell and crack, providing entry points for further infection. On fruits, brown to black, circular lesions develop on the surface. These lesions enlarge over time, become raised and rough, restricting fruit expansion and causing misshapen fruits with cracked skin exposing the inner flesh. Light exposure does not significantly affect fruit quality. Additionally, these scab spots can serve as entry points for secondary pathogens and rot development within the fruit flesh, reducing fruit quality and storage life.
organic_control: If this disease has been severe in previous seasons, applying copper-based fungicides in winter can help prevent fungal development on the tree. Sulfur applications may provide limited control against apple scab. However, sulfur-containing and pyrethrin-based solutions are viable organic options for managing this disease during the growing season.
chemical_control: If there is a possibility, always consider using an integrated approach combining preventive measures with biological treatments. Spraying preventive fungicides such as dodine, captan or dinathion around the bud and shoot wounds can help prevent this disease. When the scab disease is detected, it is possible to use fungicides containing propiconazole, myclobutanil or sulfur to control the development of this fungus. It is recommended to use different classes of fungicides when treating this disease to avoid the development of fungicide resistance.
cause: Apple scab is caused by the fungus Venturia inaequalis. This fungus survives the winter mainly on fallen infected leaves and also on bud scales and wounds on the bark of trees. In early spring, the fungus resumes growth and begins producing spores, which are then dispersed by wind. When these spores land on developing leaves and fruits, they initiate a new infection process. The outer parts of young fruit and shoots are particularly susceptible to this disease. However, mature fruits become less susceptible. Wet conditions and prolonged leaf and fruit wetness are essential factors for the development of this disease. Other host plants for this fungus include various shrubs belonging to the genus Cotoneaster, Pyracantha (firethorn) and Sorbus (rowan). All apple varieties are susceptible to this scab disease, with Gala being particularly vulnerable.
prevention: Plant resistant varieties. | Monitor the orchard for signs of the disease. | Remove and destroy infected leaves, shoots and fruits. | Clean up fallen leaves around the tree after harvest. | Another method is to spray a 5% urea solution on fallen leaves in autumn to accelerate their decomposition and hinder the life cycle of the fungus. | You can also pile up leaves to speed up their decomposition process. | Ensure proper pruning to create good air circulation within the tree canopy. | Water plants in the late afternoon or early morning and avoid watering on the foliage. | Avoid creating a humid environment under the canopy when watering plants. | Apply lime after leaf fall to increase soil pH. | Spread a layer of mulch under the tree, keeping it away from the trunk.

title: Rust Disease Affecting Beans
symptoms: The first symptoms are small, pale yellow spots on the upper surface of older leaves, typically appearing on the underside. As the disease progresses, these spots develop into raised, brownish-black pustules that rupture and release spores. The affected leaves may yellow, wilt, and fall prematurely. The disease can also affect stems and pods, leading to reduced yield and quality.
organic_control: Biological control agents such as Bacillus subtilis, Arthrobacter, and Streptomyces species have shown significant effectiveness in managing this disease.
chemical_control: In all cases, consider the possibility of applying biological prevention and treatment measures to the extent possible. Pesticides such as triazole and strobilurin are significantly effective in controlling this disease.
cause: This disease originates from a fungus with the scientific name Uromyces appendiculatus. Overwintering in plant debris left on the soil, this fungus is an obligate parasite, requiring plant tissue to survive. Initially, fungal infection occurs when fungal spores are dispersed onto plants by wind, water, and insects. This fungus thrives in conditions of high humidity and temperature. Fungal spores can proliferate very quickly under such conditions. This disease often causes serious damage during periods of hot and humid weather.
prevention: Choose to plant varieties with high resistance or tolerance. | Do not plant legumes on these The land has previously been infected with this disease. | Clear weeds and naturally growing plants from the bean field. | Avoid overwatering or using overhead sprinkler irrigation systems. | Cut and remove all infected plant parts. | After harvesting, clean up and dispose of all plant debris. | Choose a planting date and plan care and watering carefully to avoid prolonged leaf dampness in high temperatures. | Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization and ensure adequate potassium fertilization.
Summary: Tiny yellowish-brown nodules surrounded by a yellow halo appear on the underside of older leaves. | Similar symptoms may also appear on leaf petioles, stems, and fruits. | Leaves turn yellow and wilt.

title: Brown rust disease in barley
symptoms: The first symptoms appear in late winter to spring when small, round, orange-brown nodules are scattered randomly on the upper surface of the leaves. These contain spores that will increase the infection process between barley plants. Occasionally, these nodules also develop on the stems, leaf sheaths, and panicles. They are often surrounded by a yellow or green halo. Later in the season (late spring or early summer), small black nodules gradually develop on the underside of the leaves. These new sacs contain spores that will later survive on root shoots or alternative host plants to restart the cycle. Unlike the light brown nodules, the black nodules do not peel off to the fingers when rubbed.
organic_control: To date, there is no biological control solution for barley brown rust. Please contact us if you know of any.
chemical_control: If possible, always consider using a combination of methods. Preventive measures include biological treatments. Timely spraying of prothioconazole-based fungicides will generally help control rust disease. There are also many foliar fungicides available to treat rust disease in barley. For best prevention, spray them when rust disease is first detected. Additional sprays may be necessary when the season is favorable for rust disease.
cause: Symptoms caused by the fungus Puccinia hordei, one of four fungi that cause rust disease in barley. These organisms can only grow on green plants. In the case of P. hordei, it survives the summer on late shoots and alternative host plants such as grass lilies (Ornithogalum umbellatum). Warm temperatures (15° to 22°C) with high humidity and frequent rainfall create conditions for disease development, while dry, windy days support the spread of spores. Severe brown rust disease attacks barley host plants.Weakness occurs late in the season, especially if high doses of nitrogen have been applied. Early-sown crops are more severely infected than late-sown crops, especially when the weather is warm. However, brown rust in barley is rarely a problem if the crop is treated with fungicides.
prevention: Plant disease-resistant varieties, as this is the most effective way to break the pathogen's cycle. | Avoid early planting. | Monitor the field for symptoms starting in early spring. | Remove weeds and alternative host plants after harvest to prevent disease transmission.
summary: Small, orange-brown nodules on the upper surface of the leaves. | Nodules with a halo around them. | Black nodules on the underside of the leaves.

title: Late blight disease in potatoes
symptoms: Dark brown spots develop on the leaves starting from the tip or edges. In humid climates, these spots become baked-out lesions. A white fungal coating may be visible on the underside of the leaves. As the disease progresses, the entire leaf necrotizes, turns brown, and dies. Similar lesions develop on the stem and petioles. Potato tubers have grayish-green spots on the skin, and the flesh also turns brown, rendering them inedible. The rotting condition of infected fields emits a characteristic odor.
organic_control: Use copper-based fungicides before dry weather. Spraying leaves with organic coatings can also prevent infection.
chemical_control: If possible, always consider a combination of preventative and biological treatments. The use of fungicides is crucial in controlling late blight, especially in humid areas. Fungicides that come into contact with the leaves are effective. Before infection and does not cause resistance in the fungus. Fungicides containing mandipropamid, chlorothalonil, fluazinam, or mancozeb can also be used as a preventative treatment. Treating seeds before sowing with fungicides such as mancozeb is also effective.
cause: This fungus is an obligate parasite. This means it must survive the winter in leaf and tuber debris as well as on alternate host plants to survive. It enters the plant through wounds and tears in the tuber skin. Fungal spores germinate at higher temperatures in the spring and spread via wind or water. The disease is worse on cool nights (below 18°C), warm days (18-22°C), and prolonged wet conditions such as rain and fog (90% relative humidity). Under these conditions, late blight outbreaks can occur.
prevention: Use healthy seeds or more disease-resistant plants. | Ensure good ventilation and well-drained soil in the field. | Monitor the field and remove infected plants and surrounding plants. | Apply crop rotation for two to three years with non-host plants. | Destroy stray host plants growing in and around the field. | Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization. | Use plant growth regulators. | Store tubers at low temperatures and in good ventilation. | Dispose of tubers and leaf residues after harvest by burying them 0.5 meters deep or feeding them to animals.
summary: Dark brown spots on leaf tips and edges. | Spots turn into transparent lesions. | White fungus covers the underside of leaves. | Leaves wilt and die. | Grayish-green spots on potato tubers.
